topic: rezoning a historic neighborhood for mixed-use development
agent_position: The neighborhood should be rezoned to allow mixed-use development including apartments and commercial space.
scenario_context: You are speaking with Margaret, a 60-year-old lifelong resident and president of the neighborhood association. Margaret has deep roots in the community and sees the rezoning as a threat to the neighborhood's identity, culture, and long-term residents. She is not opposed to change in principle but is deeply protective of community cohesion.
npc_opening_statement: I've lived here for 35 years. I've seen what 'development' does to neighborhoods like ours — it prices people out and turns community into commodity. I'm not convinced this is any different.
difficulty_explanation: Hard — EMOTIONAL dominant (0.28). LOGICAL barely works (0.04). Long starting gap. 14-18 turns optimal. Tests sustained emotional connection.
benchmarking_value: Tests whether the model can identify an emotionally-driven NPC and pivot away from data-heavy arguments. Exposes models that default to logic regardless of audience.

positive: That's a story I haven't heard before. Which neighborhood was that?
If that's genuinely how it played out, I'd want to visit and talk to people there myself.
That's a specific enough example on {topic} that I can actually evaluate it. Tell me more about how it played out.
If someone in a situation like mine dealt with {topic} that way and it worked, I'd want to understand what they did differently.
A concrete case on {topic} is more useful than abstract arguments. That example is worth examining.
neutral: One success story doesn't erase the dozens of cautionary tales I could give you.
I've heard similar stories before that didn't pan out the way they were told.
One example on {topic} doesn't make a pattern. I'd need to see whether that case generalizes.
The anecdote about {topic} is interesting, but I've seen similar stories that didn't pan out.
Individual cases on {topic} can be misleading. What happened to the ones where it didn't work?
resistant: Anyone can cherry-pick a feel-good example. Show me the full picture.
I could tell you ten stories about communities that were promised the same thing and lost everything.
I could give you a counter-example on {topic} for every success story you tell me.
Stories about {topic} are easy to cherry-pick. Show me the full distribution of outcomes.
Anecdotes on {topic} are the most unreliable form of evidence. I need more than that.

positive: If that's what the urban planning department is saying, at least someone official is on record.

Expert consensus on {topic} carries weight — assuming the methodology is sound and the incentives are clean.

neutral: Planners and officials said the same thing about communities that were destroyed.

Authority on {topic} matters, but I'd want to know whether the expert has skin in the game.
resistant: Authorities have sided with developers over communities before. That's not reassuring.
I've seen expert opinions on {topic} shift with the political winds. That doesn't inspire confidence.
Citing authorities on {topic} is what you do when the evidence can't stand on its own.
Experts recommending things on {topic} that benefit their own field isn't independent advice.

positive: I didn't know other communities had pushed for those protections. How did they actually get enforced?
If our neighboring district managed to preserve affordability through rezoning, that's worth looking at.
I wasn't aware that so many people in comparable situations had moved on {topic}. That's worth investigating.

neutral: Other communities making the same mistake doesn't make it a good idea.
I'd want to talk to actual residents in those places, not just read the press releases.

The adoption trend around {topic} could be genuine or it could be herd behavior. I'd want to know which.
Knowing that others have moved on {topic} is useful information, but not sufficient on its own.
resistant: Every city that's done this says it worked out. You don't hear from the communities that got displaced.
Numbers from other places don't account for our specific history and demographics.
Plenty of people have followed trends on {topic} and regretted it. Popularity isn't a strategy.
What everyone else does about {topic} isn't my concern. I make my own decisions.
Social proof on {topic} is the weakest form of argument you can give me.

positive: That's a different angle. I hadn't thought about it that way.
That's an angle on {topic} I hadn't fully considered. Keep going.
neutral: I'm not sure what you're getting at. Be specific.
I'm not sure what you're arguing about {topic} specifically. Can you be more direct?
resistant: You're talking around the real issue. What happens to people like me?

positive: The housing shortage numbers are real, I'll grant you that. But the solution can't come at our expense.

The evidence you're citing on {topic} would need independent verification before I'd act on it.

_pivot_signal: What protections are actually in the proposal — legally binding ones, not just statements of intent?
